title: A household in a republic not yet settled
summary: Everything feels new and unsettled. People talk about freedom and independence, but everyday life is still hard, local, and uncertain.
dailyReality: The country says it is free, but many people are still excluded from that promise.
speakerFrame: A household trying to make sense of independence
prompt: What does this moment feel like if you are just trying to live through it?
response: People speak in big words about liberty, but daily life is still full of work, shortages, and uncertainty. The country is new, yet power still sits in old kinds of hands.
sourcesOrRationale: Interpretive snapshot grounded in the founding era and National Archives founding documents.

voices.label: Woman
voices.speakerFrame: A woman keeping a household in the early republic
voices.response: They talk about liberty in town, but my day is still measured in hauling water, mending cloth, and making food stretch. The country is new; dependence is not.

voices.label: Man
voices.speakerFrame: A man trying to make a living in a fragile republic
voices.response: Independence has changed the flag faster than it has changed ordinary power. A man still looks to local elites, credit, and harvests before he trusts any national promise.

voices.label: Public
voices.speakerFrame: A citizen hearing republican ideals from below
voices.response: The words are grand enough to stir you, but the reach of them is plainly uneven. The republic feels like an argument that has not yet decided who counts.

title: Expansion for some, coercion for others
summary: The country is spreading fast, but that does not feel the same to everyone. Some people see land and opportunity; others face slavery, removal, and loss.
dailyReality: As the republic grows, freedom expands for some and violence grows for others.
speakerFrame: Someone living inside the republic's unequal expansion
prompt: How does expansion affect ordinary life?
response: If you are on the winning side, the country can feel full of room and promise. If you are enslaved or being pushed off your land, that same growth feels like force closing in.
sourcesOrRationale: Interpretive snapshot grounded in expansion-era conflict, the Louisiana Purchase, and the Mexican-American War.

voices.label: Woman
voices.speakerFrame: A woman living where expansion redraws everyday security
voices.response: Some families speak as if the land itself were opening for them. Others watch soldiers, speculators, or slave traders turn that same expansion into fear at the door.

voices.label: Man
voices.speakerFrame: A man caught in the republic's unequal expansion
voices.response: If you hold property and stand on the right side of power, the country feels full of motion and promise. If you do not, expansion feels like someone else's future taking shape over your body.

voices.label: Public
voices.speakerFrame: A resident watching growth and coercion advance together
voices.response: The republic grows by calling itself free while forcing whole peoples into narrower ground. Public confidence and public violence rise together.

title: War and freedom in the same breath
summary: The country is being remade through war. Freedom, danger, and the power of the state are no longer distant ideas; they are part of daily life.
dailyReality: Hope grows, but so do fear, violence, and uncertainty about what the future will really bring.
speakerFrame: Someone living through war and emancipation
prompt: What changes first when the nation breaks into war?
response: The state gets closer very quickly. Soldiers, shortages, danger, and new ideas about freedom all arrive at once, and nobody can be sure what kind of country will survive.
sourcesOrRationale: Interpretive snapshot grounded in emancipation, Civil War, and Reconstruction.

voices.label: Woman
voices.speakerFrame: A woman trying to hold family life together in wartime
voices.response: Everything becomes uncertain at once: food, news, safety, and the fate of men you love. Freedom sounds larger than before, but so does the price of reaching it.

voices.label: Man
voices.speakerFrame: A man living through war and emancipation
voices.response: The state suddenly knows your name, your labour, and perhaps your body. A man can feel history moving through his street before he understands what country will come out the other side.

voices.label: Public
voices.speakerFrame: A citizen watching the nation break and reform
voices.response: War drags ideas about union and freedom out of speeches and into daily risk. Nothing about the country feels abstract once survival and citizenship are being renegotiated together.

title: Modern city, unequal republic
summary: Life feels faster, louder, and more modern. Cities grow, machines spread, and new chances appear, but inequality and segregation shape who benefits.
dailyReality: The modern world opens new doors, but it also divides neighborhoods, hardens class lines, and concentrates power.
speakerFrame: A city worker in an unequal modern age
prompt: What does modern progress feel like on the ground?
response: The city feels alive and full of motion, but it also feels crowded, unequal, and controlled by people far above you. Progress is real, but it does not arrive fairly.
sourcesOrRationale: Interpretive snapshot grounded in industrialization, segregation, and reform-era shifts.

voices.label: Woman
voices.speakerFrame: A woman navigating a crowded, unequal industrial city
voices.response: The city offers wages, lights, and movement, but also rooms packed too tight and money that vanishes too fast. Progress is easy to admire from a distance and harder to live inside.

voices.label: Man
voices.speakerFrame: A man working in the unequal modern republic
voices.response: Machines and rail lines make the country feel unstoppable. They do not stop bosses from setting the terms or neighborhoods from being sorted by power.

voices.label: Public
voices.speakerFrame: A resident watching modernity widen opportunity and control
voices.response: The republic looks richer, faster, and more capable than before. It also looks more willing to call inequality the price of progress.

title: Crisis makes the state real
summary: First the economy collapses, then war changes everything. The federal government becomes a real force in jobs, food, safety, and national purpose.
dailyReality: Relief payments, rationing, public works, and war service pull everyday life much closer to Washington.
speakerFrame: A household pulled into Depression and war mobilization
prompt: How does crisis change what government means to people?
response: Government stops feeling distant when it helps you eat, work, or survive. In hard times and war, the state becomes part of the rhythm of ordinary life.
sourcesOrRationale: Interpretive snapshot grounded in the New Deal and World War II mobilization.

voices.label: Woman
voices.speakerFrame: A woman living through depression relief and war mobilization
voices.response: When work disappears, policy stops feeling abstract very quickly. A relief check, a ration book, or a government job can mean the difference between dignity and panic.

voices.label: Man
voices.speakerFrame: A man pulled from hardship into state-led recovery and war
voices.response: The government becomes real when it helps put bread on the table and then asks for service in return. Crisis teaches you how large the state can become when the country decides it must act.

voices.label: Public
voices.speakerFrame: A citizen seeing federal power become part of ordinary life
voices.response: Washington no longer feels like a distant dome and a speech. It shows up in wages, roads, food, uniforms, and the sense that private life is tied to national survival.

title: Prosperity with a line through it
summary: The country looks richer and more stable, but that picture is incomplete. Prosperity grows, yet battles over race, rights, and war are everywhere.
dailyReality: Many homes are more comfortable, but the country is arguing more openly about justice, power, and who truly belongs.
speakerFrame: Someone living in a more comfortable but more contested America
prompt: What does this period feel like behind the polished surface?
response: There is more comfort and confidence than before, but the country does not feel settled. Questions about race, war, and belonging are now too visible to ignore.
sourcesOrRationale: Interpretive snapshot grounded in postwar life and the civil-rights era.

voices.label: Woman
voices.speakerFrame: A woman living inside postwar comfort and public conflict
voices.response: The appliances are newer and the rooms more comfortable, but comfort does not stop the arguments outside from entering the home. Race, war, and rights make the old calm harder to believe in.

voices.label: Man
voices.speakerFrame: A man in prosperous but contested America
voices.response: People talk as if the country has finally settled into success, yet the streets and television keep proving otherwise. A man can feel materially safer and politically less certain at the same time.

voices.label: Public
voices.speakerFrame: A citizen watching postwar order crack under its own claims
voices.response: This is the moment when prosperity stops hiding contradiction. The country looks strongest just as its unfinished promises become hardest to deny.

title: After trust thins out
summary: Jobs change, factories close, and trust in public life weakens. People have more consumer choice, but less sense that the country is moving together.
dailyReality: Daily life feels more individualized, more mediated, and less secure than it once did.
speakerFrame: Someone living through deindustrialization and distrust
prompt: How does it feel when the country keeps changing but trust keeps shrinking?
response: You may have more things to buy and more media to watch, but less faith that the system works for people like you. Work feels shakier, and public life feels harder to believe in.
sourcesOrRationale: Interpretive snapshot grounded in the late-20th-century market turn and post-Watergate distrust.

voices.label: Woman
voices.speakerFrame: A woman managing insecurity after work and trust thin out
voices.response: The shops are full, but steady confidence is not. You learn to plan around layoffs, rising costs, and the feeling that the institutions above you are less dependable than they sound.

voices.label: Man
voices.speakerFrame: A man living through deindustrialization and distrust
voices.response: A good job can disappear, and with it the story you told yourself about the country. Media grows louder while public faith grows weaker, and the two changes feed each other.

voices.label: Public
voices.speakerFrame: A citizen watching individual choice replace shared confidence
voices.response: You can buy more, watch more, and move more freely than before, yet public life feels thinner. The republic becomes easier to consume and harder to believe in.

title: Always online, less held together
summary: Life is faster, more online, and more exposed. People are constantly connected, but shared trust feels weaker and the country feels more fragmented.
dailyReality: You can see and say more than ever, yet still feel anxious, overloaded, and far from a common public life.
speakerFrame: A person trying to keep up in the platform era
prompt: What does this period feel like in everyday terms?
response: You are always connected, always reacting, and rarely fully at rest. Information is everywhere, but it can still feel hard to know what is true or what kind of future people share.
sourcesOrRationale: Interpretive snapshot grounded in 9/11, the financial crisis, and the platform-era public sphere.

voices.label: Woman
voices.speakerFrame: A woman trying to live in the platform-era public sphere
voices.response: The phone never really lets the day end. News, fear, work, family, and argument all arrive through the same glass and leave you feeling informed and unsettled at once.

voices.label: Man
voices.speakerFrame: A man living through platform-era acceleration
voices.response: Everything moves faster than trust can keep up. You are expected to stay available, keep reacting, and build a life on systems that feel useful one day and brittle the next.

voices.label: Public
voices.speakerFrame: A citizen watching common reality fragment
voices.response: The country is connected more tightly than ever and feels less held together. Public life no longer lacks information; it lacks shared weight and shared rhythm.
